summary: The Ozone Edge VT is the 2026 update to Ozone's legendary big air platform, featuring refined canopy shaping and updated bridle geometry for even more predictable looping and extended hangtime.
best_for: Big air riders upgrading to the latest Edge iteration

sources.summary: Early adopters note refined handling and slightly more user-friendly looping compared to the V11, while maintaining the massive boost the Edge is known for.
Ozone's team riders showcase the VT's improvements including smoother bar feel and more progressive power delivery through loops.
Discussion centers on whether the VT offers enough improvement over the V11 to justify upgrading. Consensus is it is a refinement rather than revolution.

reviews.video_title: Big Air Kite Review 21 - Ozone Edge VT

Sadly, I don't have access to a 9m version of

reviews.full_transcript: Hello and welcome to another plus-size kite review where in today's episode I will be covering the new Ozone Edge VT and I will also talk a little bit about my experience riding a couple of sessions on the 10 m version of the new Alula Edge, the Ultra X. Sadly, I don't have access to a 9m version of the Ultra X to do a more balanced and fair comparison. So, I will really just be describing my thoughts on it um without actually doing a proper review. These demo kites were lent to me by our amazing local shop here in Auckland, the New Zealand Board Store. So, if you are Auckland based, you should definitely check them out to get your hands on one for a demo. Um, but if you're abroad, particularly in the States, of course, the legends over at Matt Kite will no doubt have you covered for all your other ozone needs stateside. As usual, I was using the Surfer app for this review because I like to know how high I am jumping no matter what type of trick I am doing, particularly for my reviews and for progression. and I just find the Surf app to be the most consistent way of measuring my height, whether I'm doing a standard boost, loops, rotations, or even board offs. This ability to track your height over different styles of jumps for me is a key to progression. If you are looking to improve your own skills with a lot more technical tricks, it really helps to know how high you are going with everything and where there's room for improvements, no matter where your legs, board, or body are positioned. So, I highly recommend giving it a go if you have a smartwatch. Now, with all of that upkeep out of the way, let's get on to the kites. First of all, if you've ever gotten your hands on an Ozone kite, right out of the bag, they always tend to feel really well built and quite robust, which is always for me a bit reassuring to know you're most likely going to get a kite that is going to last you a few seasons. When you do get them up in the air, uh the first thing you'll likely notice is that the kites are very steady in the sky and well balanced in terms of the input you get from the movement it takes to pilot them. something that once used to translated uh to me landing a lot of a certain style of tricks that I struggled with over the faster kites, but then also made other maneuvers more challenging for me as well, which I will go into more detail for shortly. Compared, however, to most of the other kites I have reviewed over the last year or two, there is a fair bit more steering pressure with this kite when you are engaging the bar to move the kite around. Even more so when you're airborne. Now, not specifically just having the kite up in the air or even as you ride back and forth, but certainly when you are moving the kite around the window and changing its direction or sheeting the bar in and out to maneuver it. This kite steering pressure is going to be some of the heaviest I've reviewed since that initial video I did three years ago, falling somewhere around that of the recent Lacuna Alpha, which would make it a little bit more than the Rebels of the sort of recent kites I've tested in the heavier bar area. This of course translates to a fairly slow to respond feedback from the kite until you give it a little bit more juice, which can actually in turn be quite good as in some cases it does mean the kite stays very stable and makes it hard for you to get yourself in trouble through piloting air, particularly in stronger winds. However, after all the lighter kites I've been reviewing lately, I did find it took me a bit of getting used to in these sizes, the 9 and 10. In theory, of course, the Alula version would be a little bit easier and quicker, but because the Alula version I was trying was a size 10 and the Dacron a size 9, I found I just found the response to feedback still a little bit slower than the 9 m for the 10. So, of course, for me, it's quite hard to say just how much difference there would be in equivalent size, but I would certainly say that there would be at least a noticeable difference. and the 10 did perform very well for a 10 meter. Now, for a lot of riders who are used to edges or other heavier bar pressure kites like say the XR range from Core, then it's not going to be too hard to adapt to this. And this newer model might even be a bit lighter than you might be used to in the older ones. But even for other riders who do prefer a light to easier to maneuver kite, there are some real benefits to having heavier steering pressure for rider progression. When it comes to boosting and certain technical tricks, particularly multiple rotations because the kite tends to stay in a position more easily, which can keep yourself from getting into trouble as quickly um by accidentally pulling on the bar too much. Um, so that is something that is beneficial with these heavier bar pressured kites. When comparing the two kites, the standard VT and the Alula version, sadly, because I test them in different size, I wasn't really able to ride them on the same days either. So, it was just too hard to accurately compare the vast differences between them. However, I was able to ride the VT 9 meter backto back with the Vortex, which to date is still one of my all-time favorite kites. And one of the better features of both kites was that I was able to get out on them in relatively low wind for the size of them. And over that time, the wind ramped up quite a bit. So there were situations where lighter friends of mine were even riding larger kites from their orbits to their eras when I was on nines and they were on 10, but I was still able to jump similar heights to them and remain well powered for the whole session. I do love big air kites with a larger wind range. It's still probably my overall favorite attribute of my quiver of 2023 Evo DABS. And although I'm not going to pretend the edge is quite on that level, it is still one of the better ones I've tried recently for low-end in particular, particularly that isn't a Alula kite. One other particular item of note would be that in the best session I had with the 9 m VT where the wind was getting a lot stronger after a very enjoyable 2 hours on the kite. Sadly my elbow joints where I've had issues before with heavier kites were certainly starting to feel the work I was having to put in to use the kite. So, I got off the water for a quick refresh. And thankfully, after a little break and a stretch, they did feel sweet enough to go back out. But this time, I took out the Vortex to give that a test in comparison. And it was almost shocking how much less work the Vortex was in this mid-strength to stronger wind from 25 to 30 knots. It literally felt like the wind had dropped about three to five knots, but everything I was doing was going just as high. Uh, however, I did actually struggle with the speed of the vortex when I switched back from the VT. Um, with a lot of my jumps being over and the timing on my loops with the faster kite not being quite as consistent as the VT. So, it was a noticeable comparison how much uh adjustment that did take for me on a kite that I previously was fairly familiar with. Certainly some food for thought for anyone else trying kites randomly for short periods of time. Some kites are just different enough from what you're used to that even a short demo won't always give you the best idea what the kite has to offer until you've really spent some time on it and adjusted it to your riding style. Needless to say, sore arms aside, I really enjoyed every session I had on both the nine and 10 meters of the Edge. And once I was clever enough to adjust my timing and style of riding to them, um, and what I found them best for, um, I really enjoyed it and got quite a lot out of them. So, let's have a look at each kite's performance in the key categories, starting with boosting and hang time. Now, historically, the Edge has always been one of the bigger boosting kites, and in my first review, I found it to be right up near my old XR7s at the time. So, with so many kites, however, now pushing the boundaries of design and material, I do think a lot of the field has caught up um to some extent at least uh at least in the performance potential of them. For sure, this kite still has a lot of power, but that power does come in a fairly easy to access sheet and go nature versus a lot of the kites in the field. But, as I mentioned just a moment ago, I wasn't finding the edge helped me get any higher than say the Vortex uh because I was able to shoot the Vortex a lot quicker and it possibly caught a little bit more gusts with the way it sits further forward with the ULA. Um, but I did find just in general the edge VT to be a little bit easier to consistently go high. When compared to how much less effort, however, the Vortex needed to send the kite and the general strain on my arms. Of course, I still did prefer it once I had adjusted back to its speed. However, I certainly could imagine a lot of riders preferring the Edge not only in the 9, but more so in the 7 or 8 m sizes where the slightly less twitchy and responsive nature the Vortex or another faster kite would have wouldn't be uh as valuable in the stronger woods on a smaller kite. Now, in terms of hang time, both kites had a decent amount if you were to just send it up and park it to one side, but with how stable this kite is, uh, likely from the combination of the heavier construction being five struts and also having the eightpoint bridles, I did find it quite challenging to move it through the sky very quickly while airborne, particularly when I would ever sheet out the bar a little it um or reduce tension on the lines. Uh I tend to do this by habit to position the kite above my head uh on my descents to either initiate heli loops or to go for board offs. I struggled so much with the boardoffs that despite riding both the 9 and 10 m in multiple sessions, I wasn't even able to land one clean one despite a dozen or more combined attempts over those sessions. I kept finding that when I would go to put the board back on, I would struggle to do a one-handed heloop, and I would usually find myself plopping down in the water without quite getting the board back on my feet. This was surprising to me, more so for the 10 m Alula version, which I would have thought would be a great kite for my basic board off tricks, but I just couldn't seem to land any with the kite. to the kite's defense. My skills in the board off arena are still pretty weak. Um, and there are some days that I just can't seem to find my footing, so to speak, by getting the board back on, but I usually can almost always land at least one or two with all the kites I'm testing. Now, when it comes to looping, you might think that stability uh I mentioned gives it some real problems, but on the contrary, I actually found the kites both really good for loops once I overcame my hesitation from the bar pressure. Uh, I found that as long as I was maintaining line tension through the jump up until the point when I pulled on the bar to send the kite, the edges were quite responsive in the air while moving the kite. And it would go straight through the loops nice and cleanly. And because the kites were moving at fairly good speed, I found timing them very easy to complete the loop smoothly and even initiate some kite loop rotation tricks from smaller boogies to back rolls and even some late backs. So for riders who are already proficient in loops and not going to be turned off by the heavier bar feel um particularly with powerful kites, this is going to work just great for them in any of the sizes up to at least 9 for the VT and 10 m for the illusion Alula version, which I did find more responsive and a little quicker relative to its size. Of course, um riders to look to get into their first loops. Well, of course, that barb pressure, um the kite's stability and its slower nature going through the loop may find this one a challenging kite to get in their first loops, at least on the same size as I was using. So, I would certainly suggest uh if they're looking to do some loops, look for the seven or 8 m in the edges if you're wanting to try your first loops. But if you are looking to get something from 9 m and up, particularly from Ozone, then I would look to the Vortex because it is already one of the best loop progression kites on the market and it is not very intimidating at all to try those first ones. So, when it comes to confidence building, well, this is also going to be a little bit of a mixed bag because, as I mentioned, for straight boosting, it's quite an easy kite. and even for progressing with rotation tricks where you want to keep the kite nice and stable above your head. Once again, this kite is fairly ideal for that in a smaller size. But if you are wanting to progress to more technical tricks or loops, then that bar pressure and stability through the sky are likely going to add some challenges to overcome. The beauty of its low-end powers do mean you can get out in lighter winds than some other kites. So that always helps when you're trying new things. Um, and it is certainly a big boost in adding new skills when you're not feeling overpowered. And this kite's added stability means that when the wind starts to get pretty gnarly, at least you'll be able to maintain your control. Um although the bar pressure might have a habit of making the conditions feel more intense than they would on some other kites of course. So in summary, this is a really fun kite for the style of riding it offers with solid sheet and go power for all levels. And despite some challenges, it would be an ideal addition for a lot of riders uh with ozone or other low V quivers where you can share your bars on multiple kites. When I rode the Edge a few years ago in my first review, I did remember thinking this would be a great addition to my Orbit quiver in a size seven or 8 m specifically for jumping and um trying to get height in stronger winds because of the added power and stability. And I think that still holds up to some extent. If I were building an all ozone big air quiver, I'd have to look to go for an edge in the 7 or 8 m. Then I would be jumping onto the Vortex in sizes 9 and up. In terms of the VT versus the ULA, um it's a bit hard to say just how much more you are getting there because of the limit time I've had on them and them being different kite sizes. But as you may have recalled me saying in other reviews, I do find that Alula tends to bring the best value for money in three strut kites. Mainly because of the added performance across the wind range, whereas with five struts, although they certainly tend to have an added performance, it's not quite as much because the five strut kites are already fairly uh good in strong winds and are super stable and boosty. If I am ever able to get hold of a 9m Ultra X of the Edge, then I'll do a follow-up video comparing them, of course. But for now, this is already one of my preferred Dacron kites so far this year, and it's definitely worth a try if you can get your hands on one. So, thanks once again to NZ Board Store for these demos, and be sure to like and subscribe if you haven't already. Perhaps share this video in your local kite groups, and leave a comment. what kite you're riding at the moment. Um, or leave a kite for me to look at what I should be reviewing next. Um, there's still a couple months left in our windy season and I've got some time on my hands to do some more reviews. So, until next time, goodbye. [Music]
